sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Non-traumatic dental pain is usually odontogenic and commonly caused by caries, pulpitis, periodontal disease, erupting teeth, or dental abscess. Prehospital care should focus on excluding serious spread of infection, providing symptom relief within scope, and directing the patient to definitive dental care.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Localised toothache, sensitivity to hot, cold, or chewing
- Gum swelling, local erythema, or bad taste in the mouth
- Facial swelling or trismus in more severe infection
- Fever, malaise, dysphagia, or voice change if infection is spreading

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Definitive care is dental treatment rather than repeated analgesia alone
- Red flags include spreading facial swelling, trismus, dysphagia, drooling, voice change, floor-of-mouth swelling, systemic toxicity, or airway concern
- Atypical jaw or dental pain can be referred from sinus, TMJ, or cardiac pathology
- Immunocompromise, diabetes, and delayed access to dental care increase the need for escalation planning

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and focused vital sign survey
- Inspect the mouth where safe and tolerated and assess for swelling, trismus, drooling, or voice change
- Consider simple analgesia within scope
- Cold compresses may help external swelling
- Advise urgent dental follow-up for most cases, and same-day review for suspected abscess, worsening pain, or facial swelling

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Stronger analgesia may be considered within scope or after senior consultation for severe pain
- Antibiotics should be reserved for suspected spreading infection, systemic features, or where definitive dental care is delayed and a doctor directs treatment
- Escalate urgently for airway risk, deep space infection, or sepsis
- Transport or senior consultation should not be delayed by repeated attempts at local symptom control when infection is clearly spreading

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Salt-water mouth rinses may offer temporary symptomatic relief if tolerated
- Codeine should not be relied upon as superior first-line dental analgesia compared with appropriate non-opioid options
